3.3 Average Velocity and Instantaneous Velocity>Key Concept: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of a body at a specific instant in time.
3.6 Graphical Representation of Motion>Area under v-t graph

mini_headings_covered: Key Concept: A frame of reference is a set of coordinates that can be used to determine positions of objects.
Table 3.1 Difference between distance and displacement
Key Concept: Motion is the change in position
Key Concept: Velocity is the rate of change of displacement.
Key Concept: Average velocity is the total displacement of a body over a time interval.
Key Concept: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of a body at a specific instant in time.
Key Concept: Acceleration is the rate of change of velocity.
Key Concept: Motion at a constant velocity or uniform motion means that the position of the object is changing at the same rate.

subject: PHYSICS
unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: A car accelerates from rest to 20 m/s in 10 seconds. What is the acceleration?
questions.hint: a = (v_f − v_i)/t. Starting from rest means v_i = 0.
questions.explanation: a = (20 − 0)/10 = 20/10 = 2 m/s². Option B would be 10/20 = 0.5 (incorrect inversion).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: A car accelerates from rest to 20 m/s in 10 seconds. Acceleration = 2 m/s²

explanations_tiered.beginner: The car goes from 0 to 20 m/s in 10 s → gains 2 m/s each second → a = 2 m/s².
misconceptions: Dividing time by velocity (10/20 = 0.5).

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: Between which two points on a speed-time graph is a car experiencing uniform motion?
questions.hint: Uniform motion means constant velocity, so speed does not change.
questions.explanation: On a speed-time graph, constant speed is represented by a horizontal line (zero slope). Uniform motion requires constant velocity (both speed and direction constant). Option A correctly identifies the horizontal segment.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Figure shows speed vs time graph. Between which two points is car experiencing uniform motion? (Between B and C, etc.)

explanations_tiered.definition: Uniform motion = constant velocity = constant speed on speed-time graph.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: A horizontal line on a speed-time graph indicates constant speed (zero acceleration).
explanations_tiered.beginner: If the speed line is flat (horizontal), the car is going steady — that's uniform motion.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Uniform motion ⇒ a = 0 ⇒ slope of v-t graph = 0 ⇒ horizontal line.
explanations_tiered.advanced: v(t) = constant ⇒ dv/dt = 0 ⇒ horizontal line on v-t graph.
reasoning_steps: On v-t graph, constant v = horizontal line.
misconceptions: Positive slope = uniform motion. Incorrect; positive slope = constant positive acceleration.
Any straight line = uniform motion. Incorrect; sloping lines mean changing speed.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Uniform motion: horizontal line on v-t graph.

flashcard.front: What part of v-t graph shows uniform motion?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Horizontal line = constant speed = uniform motion.

visual_system.visual_title: Speed vs Time for Uniform Motion
visual_system.visual_description: A horizontal line on a speed-time graph, indicating constant speed over time.
visual_system.caption: Horizontal line = constant speed = uniform motion.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Speed-time graph with a horizontal line from 2 to 8 seconds at 20 m/s, showing constant speed.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: v-t graph: uniform motion represented by?
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Horizontal line = constant speed.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: A man walks 7 km in 2 hours and 2 km in 1 hour in the same direction. What is the average velocity for the whole journey?
questions.hint: Average velocity = total displacement / total time. Direction is same for both segments.
questions.explanation: Total displacement = 7 km + 2 km = 9 km in the same direction. Total time = 2 h + 1 h = 3 h. v_avg = 9/3 = 3 km/h in that direction. Options B and C give 2 km/h (average speed if direction ignored incorrectly).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Man walks 7 km in 2 h and 2 km in 1 h same direction. Average velocity = 3 km/h same direction

explanations_tiered.definition: Average velocity = total displacement / total time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Same direction means displacement adds directly.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Total distance = 9 km, total time = 3 h, average = 3 km/h in that direction.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: ΔS = 7 + 2 = 9 km, Δt = 2 + 1 = 3 h, v_avg = 9/3 = 3 km/h in same direction.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vector addition: displacements in same direction add algebraically.
worked_solution: Step 3: v_avg = 9/3 = 3 km/h in that direction.
misconceptions: Using harmonic mean for speeds (not needed since same direction).
Forgetting to include direction in answer.

flashcard.front: 7 km in 2 h + 2 km in 1 h same direction: average velocity?
flashcard.flashcard_back: 3 km/h in that direction (9 km/3 h).

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Total displacement = 9 km, total time = 3 h, v_avg = 3 km/h same direction.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.flashcard_front: 7 km in 2 h + 2 km in 1 h same direction: average velocity?
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Contrast with average speed if directions were different.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate average velocity for multi-segment motion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: How far does a student walk in 1.5 hours if her average speed is 5 m/s?
questions.hint: Convert time to seconds: 1.5 h = 5400 s. Distance = speed × time.
questions.explanation: t = 1.5 × 3600 = 5400 s. d = 5 m/s × 5400 s = 27000 m = 27 km. Option B would be 5 × 1.5 = 7.5 (using hours incorrectly).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: How far does a student walk in 1.5 hrs if her average speed is 5 m/s? Distance = 27 km

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Need consistent units: convert hours to seconds.
explanations_tiered.beginner: 1.5 hours = 5400 seconds. 5 m/s × 5400 s = 27000 m = 27 km.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Unit conversion essential: 1 h = 3600 s, 1 km = 1000 m.
worked_solution: Step 1: Convert time: 1.5 h × 3600 s/h = 5400 s.
misconceptions: Multiplying 5 × 1.5 = 7.5 km (forgetting unit conversion).
Using 1.5 × 1000 = 1500 m (incorrect time conversion).
explanations_tiered.revision_note: d = v × t, ensure consistent units.

flashcard.front: 5 m/s for 1.5 h: distance in km?

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Calculate distance from speed and time with unit conversion

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.subsection: Key Concept: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of a body at a specific instant in time.
questions.structural_anchor: Key Concept: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of a body at a specific instant in time.
questions.question: What is instantaneous velocity?

options.A: Velocity at a specific instant in time
options.B: Total displacement divided by total time
options.C: Average velocity over a long interval

questions.explanation: Instantaneous velocity is defined as the velocity of an object at a particular instant. Option B is average velocity, not instantaneous.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.subsection_anchor: Key Concept: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of a body at a specific instant in time.
source_trace.structural_element: Key Concept: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of a body at a specific instant in time.
source_trace.content_anchor: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of a body at a specific instant in time.

explanations_tiered.definition: Instantaneous velocity is the velocity of an object at a precise moment in time.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: It is the limit of average velocity as the time interval approaches zero.
explanations_tiered.beginner: The speedometer reading at a specific moment is your instantaneous velocity (including direction).
explanations_tiered.advanced: It is the derivative of position with respect to time: \(\vec{v} = d\vec{S}/dt\).
reasoning_steps: Consider a very short time interval around the instant.
Calculate velocity over that interval and take the limit.
misconceptions: Instantaneous velocity is average over whole trip. Incorrect; that's average velocity.
Instantaneous speed and velocity are always same magnitude? Yes for magnitude, but velocity includes direction.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Instantaneous velocity: at a specific instant.

flashcard.flashcard_back: Velocity at a specific instant in time.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Instantaneous velocity is at a specific instant.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Definition of instantaneous velocity.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: If a car is moving east and its acceleration is also east, what happens to the car's speed?
questions.hint: Acceleration in the same direction as velocity increases speed.
questions.explanation: When acceleration is in the same direction as velocity, the object speeds up. Here both are east, so speed increases. Option B would occur if acceleration was opposite velocity (west).

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Acceleration occurs when velocity changes in magnitude or direction. If in same direction as velocity, speed increases.

explanations_tiered.definition: Acceleration in same direction as velocity causes speeding up.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Parallel acceleration and velocity vectors increase speed.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you push a car forward (acceleration) while it's moving forward, it goes faster.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: When \(\vec{a}\) and \(\vec{v}\) are in same direction, the magnitude of velocity increases.
reasoning_steps: If same → speed increases; if opposite → speed decreases.
misconceptions: Acceleration always means speeding up. Incorrect; it can mean slowing down or changing direction.
If acceleration is east, car must be moving east. Not necessarily; could be moving west and slowing down.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Same direction → speeding up. Opposite → slowing down.

glossary_entry.definition: Acceleration in same direction as velocity.

flashcard.front: Car moving east, acceleration east: speed?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Acceleration in same direction as velocity → speed increases.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Relate direction of acceleration to speed change
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. a and v same direction → speeding up.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: What is the acceleration of an object in uniform motion?
questions.explanation: Uniform motion means constant velocity, so there is no change in velocity. Acceleration = change in velocity / time = 0. Options B and C would change the speed.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: The acceleration is zero (a=0) because neither the magnitude of the velocity nor its direction changes.

explanations_tiered.definition: Uniform motion = constant velocity → zero acceleration.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: If velocity does not change, acceleration is zero.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you're going steady in a straight line, you're not accelerating — a = 0.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Newton's first law: object in uniform motion has zero net force and zero acceleration.
reasoning_steps: Recall that acceleration measures change in velocity.
If velocity is constant, change = 0 → acceleration = 0.
misconceptions: Uniform motion can have constant acceleration. Incorrect; constant velocity means a=0.
Uniform motion requires constant speed but can turn. Incorrect; direction must also be constant.
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Constant velocity → zero acceleration.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Uniform motion = constant velocity → a=0.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: What physical quantity is represented by the area under a velocity-time graph?
questions.hint: Area under v-t = ∫ v dt = displacement.
questions.explanation: The area under a velocity-time graph equals the displacement (change in position). For uniform motion, area = rectangle = v × t = displacement. Option B is the slope, not area.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: The area under the v-t graph in a uniform motion represents the distance covered by the object.

explanations_tiered.concept_summary: For uniform motion, area = rectangle = v × t = displacement.
explanations_tiered.beginner: If you go 10 m/s for 5 s, the rectangle area (50) is how far you went.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: \(\Delta S = \int_{t_1}^{t_2} v(t) dt\) = area under v-t curve.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Displacement is the integral of velocity with respect to time.
reasoning_steps: For a graph, area under curve gives product.
misconceptions: Area under v-t = acceleration. Incorrect; slope = acceleration.
Area = distance always? For straight-line motion without reversal, area = displacement magnitude = distance.
explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Area under v-t = displacement.

visual_system.visual_description: A rectangle on a velocity-time graph with area = v × t, representing displacement.
visual_system.caption: Shaded area = displacement.
visual_system.accessibility_alt_text: Velocity-time graph with horizontal line at 10 m/s from 0 to 5 s. Shaded rectangle area = 10 × 5 = 50 m = displacement.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Emphasize that slope = acceleration, area = displacement.
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Area under v-t = displacement.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: What is the purpose of the PhET 'Moving Man' simulation mentioned in the unit?

options.A: To visually explore motion graphs by controlling a character's movement
options.B: To calculate the mass of moving objects
options.C: To measure the speed of light

questions.hint: The simulation is about motion in a straight line.
questions.explanation: The PhET 'Moving Man' simulation allows students to control a character's motion and see the corresponding position-time and velocity-time graphs. Option A correctly describes its purpose. The other options are unrelated.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Click on link to perform virtual laboratory on motion in straight line. The Moving Man PhET Experiment

explanations_tiered.definition: PhET simulation is an interactive tool for understanding motion graphs.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: The Moving Man simulation lets users control motion and see real-time graph changes.
explanations_tiered.beginner: You can move a stick figure and watch how the position and velocity graphs change.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: The simulation provides visual feedback connecting motion to position-time and velocity-time graphs.
explanations_tiered.advanced: It allows exploration of concepts like constant velocity, acceleration, and graphical interpretation.
reasoning_steps: Recall that the simulation is for motion in one dimension.
It links motion to graphical representations.
misconceptions: The simulation is for advanced physics only. Incorrect; it's for introductory kinematics.
It measures real-world distances. Incorrect; it's a simulation.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Moving Man: explore motion and graphs interactively.

flashcard.flashcard_back: Motion in a straight line with real-time position and velocity graphs.

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The simulation helps explore motion and graphs interactively.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.revision_note: Moving Man: visualize motion and graphs.
publishing_metadata.teacher_note: Encourage students to use the simulation for better understanding.
publishing_metadata.learning_objective_tag: Use virtual laboratory to explore motion
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. The simulation helps understand motion graphs by controlling a character.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
questions.question: Which of the following is a scalar quantity?
questions.hint: Scalars have magnitude only; vectors have magnitude and direction.
questions.explanation: Distance is a scalar quantity because it has only magnitude. Displacement, velocity, and acceleration are vectors because they include direction. Option A is correct.

source_trace.pdf_unit: Unit 3: Motion in a Straight Line
source_trace.content_anchor: Distance is a scalar quantity. Displacement is a vector quantity.

explanations_tiered.definition: Scalar quantities have only magnitude; vectors have magnitude and direction.
explanations_tiered.concept_summary: Distance is scalar; displacement, velocity, acceleration are vectors.
explanations_tiered.beginner: Distance just tells 'how much', not 'which way' — it's a scalar.
explanations_tiered.intermediate: Scalars: distance, speed, mass, time. Vectors: displacement, velocity, acceleration.
explanations_tiered.advanced: Vector quantities obey vector algebra; scalars obey ordinary algebra.
reasoning_steps: If yes → vector; if no → scalar.
misconceptions: Velocity is scalar. Incorrect; velocity includes direction.
Distance is sometimes vector. Incorrect; distance is always scalar.
explanations_tiered.revision_note: Scalar: magnitude only (distance). Vector: magnitude + direction.

flashcard.front: Which is scalar: distance or displacement?

explanations_tiered.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Distance is scalar; displacement, velocity, acceleration are vectors.

publishing_metadata.flashcard_deck: Physics Unit 3 — Motion in a Straight Line
publishing_metadata.solution_manual_note: Answer A. Distance is scalar; others are vectors.
